speaker: interviewer
text: And the tape is rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And you said that there was no organization set up for you to exchange the documents?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no organization set up at that point, unless you could get to Kuybyshev, a city on the Volga, where the Polish govern-- where the Russian government had been evacuated from Moscow, and where the Polish Embassy was. But that was difficult to get there. We got into a place called Asbest, A-S-B-E-S-T. It's the center of the Soviet Russian asbestos industry. It is about two hours by train from what the city, which we call-- was called Sverdlovsk. It's now back Yekaterinburg. It's the place where the last Czar was murdered by the soviets. We got into Asbest. By misinformation, I thought I was going to be able to get a job in the ceramics industry, which wasn't so. We worked all kinds of jobs over there, building trades and that, shoveling snow around the factory, and as a mechanic's apprentice, setting up punch presses and doing all kinds of things. My father was also working there. And that was really the worst place. And twice from there, we were sent to farms, in summer once, and once in fall, to help with the harvest, which was good, because at least there, we had enough to eat. While we were in the city for a long time, we were starving, until, for the first time, help came from the American Poles. There came food supplies, and there came clothing. The clothing mostly had come from England. I think the food supplies, a lot of it, had come from England, but, but most, some of it from America. There was a big colony of Poles and Jews from Poland there, and luckily, the Poles had elected a physician, a Dr. [? Mark ?] [? Zuckerman-- ?] who was from the [INAUDIBLE] a sandwich I had mentioned once-- who was the chief distributor of those things. And the reason he was helpful, because there came medication also with it. And with some of that medication, he bribed a Soviet minister who was in that town, transferred from Moscow, and the people at the hospital. The minister's wife was sick, and Dr. [? Zuckerman ?] was called to help her. And so that helped a little bit with the authorities in that town. And so until that help had arrived, we were absolutely miserable. We lived for a long time in dormitories, where the only room you had was the room beneath your bed. The sanitary conditions were lousy. Food was scarce. We were swollen from hunger. I had frostbites on both hands. There was-- our clothing was torn by then already. So when the stuff came-- the Polish help came, it really-- we started to live a little bit better. We also had, while we worked on the farm, stolen enough peas, while we were harvesting peas, to support us for a couple months with some additional food. So that helped. But the Polish army, which had been created under General [? Anders, ?] soon left the Soviet Union during the Battle of Stalingrad. And besides, they had taken on only a limited number of Jews. We found out, later on, why the Soviets had made that demand. They had not specified Jews. They had specified other minorities and Poles, and they were afraid, mainly afraid, that people from the territories which they had occupied in 1939 from the [? Polish perimeter, ?] that Ukrainians and white Russians would try to join the Polish army in order to get rid of the Soviets. Well, in 1943, the Germans discovered the graves at [? Katin ?], accused the Russians of having murdered thousands of Polish officers. And I won't mention the number, because numbers we hear aren't very different. It varies between 5,000 and 10,000. The Soviets said they were murdered by the Russians-- by the Germans. The Polish exiled government in London demands an explanation, which drives the Soviets mad. And first of all, they close all the temporary consulates, which, in the meantime, had opened, but were called, still, only delegations of the embassy. They arrested their heads, and they called all those people, like us, who had those temporary documents not exchanged into passports to the police and told them, from today on, you are Soviet citizens. And within a short time, we were given some-- the explanations there were were ridiculous. They said, you were in Tarnopol at the day when there was an election. I said, we were not, did not vote on that election. They said, that doesn't mean anything, but you were there when the people of Tarnopol voted to be united with the Soviet Union.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, not only was I-- the moment I became a Soviet Citizen, against my will, I also became eligible for the draft. As long as I was a Polish citizen, I was not. And so shortly later, about a few weeks later, we are called up to the draft board. I am given, being given 1A, being a young boy, 22-years-old, reasonably healthy, and my father, 4F.

speaker: interviewee
text: A few weeks later, I am called to the office-- military office there, and after a lengthy interview with an officer, at which time I was asked if I would serve in the army, I said, if I'm needed, I go. And at which point, I had-- at the one repeated questions, we said-- when I-- what my nationality was. Now, one must understand, to the Russians, nationality does not mean citizenship, but it means, really, ethnicity. And that ethnicity, in Russia, is always listed in your identity card. Once a Jew, once a Pole, once a-- whatever, Chinese, you are-- you and all your heirs are going to be the same. Well, I stated-- we had stated when we had received our temporary Russian documents, that our ethnicity was Polish. Going out from the idea that, if the war ends, if we say we are Jewish, religion hasn't been asked. Ethnicity is being asked. If we say that we are Jewish, then when the war ends we have to stay there, even if there is a Poland. If we say we are Polish, we can go back to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this way I got-- when I said that I go in the army, and I was told two days later to report, I didn't know yet what army I'm going to. It wasn't until two days later, when I reported at 10 o'clock in the evening to that office, that the officer told me where to go, whom to find at the railroad station, and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then finally, he said, and if somebody asks you where you are going, tell him you're going to the 1st Polish Kosciuszko Infantry Division. And good luck, and behave yourself with dignity. And off I went. On June 5th, after all kinds of manipulations, being in a collection point and put on a train and travelling-- on June 5th, we arrived at a railroad station not far from the town of [? Railzai ?], the city of-- the railroad station [? Devovo ?]. There was only one railroad car at the station building. The station had been bombed. Nothing else was there. And we walked about 15 kilometers and crossed a river, Oka-- O-K-A-- on a ferry, which was pulled by a string by two men over the river, and got into a village called Sielce-- S-I-E-L-C-E-- where the Poles 1st Division was being created. Us, we-- everybody had to walk through a narrow entrance, and there was a fellow in uniform standing there-- Polish uniform-- and he asked everybody for his name. And he, obviously-- I recognized, was Jewish-- and when he saw the name 'Guttmann,' he knew I was Jewish. And he said, you have to write an application that you want to serve in the Polish army or in Polish Division as a volunteer, which I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I had arrived, and my unit-- I was assigned to a unit that same day-- and then I arrived in my unit without any papers. And the first thing, when they were writing down all our data, I am asked, what is your ethnicity? And I say, Jewish, because now I am in the Polish army, and I don't want to stay there forever. So when the war comes to an end, as a Jew, if they don't kick me out, at least I will be able to get out, which was all right. I stayed with the same unit from June 5, 1940-- between 1943, sorry-- from June 5, 1943, until after the end of the war, until we returned into our barracks in Poland. For the last eight months of my service, I was transferred to another unit in, in the [? border guards. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: I went with that unit from Sielce on the Oka, first, through the area of Smolensk, where we became like a third row of defense. From there, we moved to the Ukraine in the area of-- between two Jewish towns, [INAUDIBLE] and [? Berdychiv, ?] in a little village at the [INAUDIBLE]. From there, we went to a place in the Ukraine near the city of Luck-- L-U-C-K-- in an area full of anti-Soviet guerrillas, where we had to watch ourselves terribly. We lost-- our regiment subsequently lost a truck with its crew over there. We never recovered either the crew nor the truck. And from there, in June of-- beginning of July, 1944 we went into action. My regiment was an under-20-millimeter-mortar regiment. That's an-- our artillery was an artillery shell, weighing 16 pounds, with a reach of 6 kilometers. We fired 38,000 rounds of ammunition during the period when I served with them, and I kept the statistics, so I know what we did. I wrote the history of the regiment after the war for the army, for the Ministry of Defense in Warsaw. And we participated in the fights from the river [INAUDIBLE] to the Vistula, to the right bank of the Vistula in Warsaw and the left bank of Vistula, in January of 1945, to [? Pomerania, ?] the Baltic Sea at Kolberg, across the [? Oda ?] River, participated in the surrounding of Berlin, never got into Berlin proper while we were fighting, and to the [? Elbe ?] River, where we were when the war-- No. Where we were-- three days and then we were withdrawn to a place near Berlin called [INAUDIBLE] where we were when-- on May 8th, when the war came to an end.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were fighting, did you know what was happening to the Jews of Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, we were fighting against Hitler. It was-- we didn't know what happened to the Jews of Poland. Nobody knew at that time. We knew when we came into Poland. We found out more. But when we joined in Russia, we didn't know what was going on. The last time I heard from my mother was probably through a Red Cross card sometimes in 1941 or 1942, when we were in Asbest. That was the last time we heard something from her. By then she was in [? Sosnowiec ?] in the ghetto. Otherwise, we didn't know. Once we came to Poland, we found out a little bit more. The whole [? colossality ?] of that holocaust, of that became, we became aware only after the war, after I  was discharged.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was only after.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because in-- you see, don't forget, it's 1945. There's no television. Papers are sporadic. Mail is going sporadic. You got an army paper while you're still a soldier. Radio-- in the army, even though there were military apparatuses, we were not allowed to listen to civilian stations. We didn't always obey. We heard a little bit-- especially tried to listen to the BBC, which was, even in later years, much better always than the voice of America, by the way. And so it took time for all that to find out what had all happened. I had befriended a girl in Warsaw-- near Warsaw, with whom I was corresponding. Mail for a soldier is a terribly important thing. She knew that I was Jewish, and she wrote me, while I was in Germany that she had found out some Jews survived in Poland. And she asked for more information on my mother, and she said she was going to look for her. She didn't find anything. I found that, upon my return to Poland, by coincidence, that there is a Jewish committee in the city of. Katowice. And without going into the details, I got there and finally, I found out that in that committee after-- which was not the best organized at the time because people were amateurs. They didn't know how to do things like that. They were trying. Everybody-- everybody, wherever you were during that war, Jews, very fast, were able to organize committees to help. But they had to learn some of the things. And then I found out that my mother and my sister had survived at a labor camp in the district of Lower Silesia, not far from [? Breslau ?] [INAUDIBLE]. And the camp was known as Peterswaldau, and it was a branch of the notorious Gross-Rosen concentration camp. That was an exclusively women's camp. Not far away was a men's camp in Langenbielau which had the nickname [GERMAN]. My mother and sister worked in a metal factory there, a factory which was making bomb mechanisms. Except for the last couple weeks before the end of the war, they were chased out to dig trenches. But that's where they survived. They subsequently came to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father-- your father didn't survive the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father died in February of 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he die from?

speaker: interviewee
text: He died of a bad combination of pneumonia, under-nourishment, and heartache. He was alone, he got sick, he was in the hospital. I didn't-- I had been writing to him. As a matter of fact, I had also-- when we came to the Polish army we were-- to the Polish Division-- we were told that there is, in Moscow, a committee which is called the committee of Polish Patriots, which was instrumental in creating, that second time. The Polish army. They would help our relatives if they needed help. In December of 1943, I was on a special mission as a courier. I passed through Moscow, and I had about 12 hours' layover, and I went to the committee in my uniform. I reported there, where it was. And the lady who opened the door and talked to me when I said my name said, oh yeah, I remember you. That is such a German name. And what she told me was, well, we are going to check. We don't have an office in Asbest. We are going to check through our office in some other town. I have to check with the social department in that city. If your father needs help, and if the social department informs the city in Kirov that you need it, and Kirov informs us, then we're going to-- I mean, you know. It was such a way. I don't know if my father ever got any help. I didn't have any mail from him for months, until in June of 1944, I finally got a letter from the first secretary of the party in that factory where my father worked. I had written to the doctor. The doctor never answered me. I remembered the name of this party secretary, because she was a former wife of the Russian foreign minister, [INAUDIBLE]. She was divorced, and she was first secretary of the party in that factory producing military goods. And that's a name you remember. And the name of the factory, to give-- without a street address, didn't, in that little town of Asbest, make a problem easier. So I wrote a letter to Madam [INAUDIBLE] because now I was in the army. I was a big shot. I could call [INAUDIBLE] comrade. [LAUGHING] [? Litvinova. ?] And I got a letter from her. And she told me when my father passed away. I was able to obtain a certificate of that from the doctor whom I found two days before he left Poland, and he sent it to me. And my mother needed it later on when she got, when she remarried, here, in the United States, another refugee from Hungary who had lost his wife and four children at Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were in the army, did you ever come across any of these notorious camps that were scattered through Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only camp I really passed by was Majdanek. At the time, I wasn't aware what it is. We were at, that was-- there was a first few weeks when we were in action in July of 1944. We had crossed the [? Volga ?] River. We had been greeted enthusiastically by the Poles when we came through there. It was a sunny day, and we-- suddenly, there's a whole convoy stop, because somewhere, always, a convoy gets into some trouble, and something happens. And we stopped just in front of a gate, which I remember, an iron gate which was open. There was a fence surrounding the place, and I saw some construction, which turned out to be ovens from which there still was smoke coming out. It was only later that we found out that it was a notorious camp, Majdanek. When you stop, sitting high on a truck, and see something, you really don't know yet what it is until somebody later tells you what it was. So that was the camp, there. We came through another-- not this kind of camp. An encampment where the Germans had been training foreign police-- Lithuanian, Estonian, Polish, from all those occupied countries where they needed policemen. We came in that camp that was near the city town of Pulawy-- P-U-L-A-W-Y-- on the Vistula, in a little village, there, outside in the forest. There were-- some of the barracks had been destroyed. There were still peeled potatoes in the kitchen, in the big, huge vats where they were supposed to be cooked. And I found, in there is a volume of interesting documents from a one policeman, who apparently had joined, then was reluctant and escaped. And there was such a volume of paperwork with him escaping and being caught and escaping and caught. And that was-- but that was the first time we also, I realized that the Germans had been training foreign policemen, in the war. Other camps, I did not come by until after the war. I passed by quite often at Auschwitz, because there was an important railroad going there, which I took to go to some villages in the mountains during vacation time, so-- but otherwise, I did not come. We were not too far from Stutthof, apparently. And as I-- at one point, the first Jews after the war who had survived were a group of Jewish youngsters. I mean, youngsters-- they were in their late teens, beginning twenties probably-- who had survived. I believe, it was Stutthof. I met, later on, quite a few of them in Katowice and Sosnowiec-- in [INAUDIBLE] and Sosnowiec. They were in [? kibbutz, ?] kind of, preparing to go to Israel. And I spent the high holidays in 1945 in [INAUDIBLE] at a kibbutz, after a lot of haggling with my military commander, who wouldn't give me off a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that when board the train, before you went to Siberia, they took off all the bachelors, and you never found out what happened to them until 1943. What happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were taken, first of all, in Tarnopol, to the jail, and it was a terrible-- There was, like, an old fortress. From there, they were taken on trains, which-- the kind which Solzhenitsyn describes in his where each fellow sits in a little, separate cell so that he can communicate with the others. In wagons like this, they were taking-- and they were taking to camps which-- in Siberia, we were not surrounded by a fence. There was a forest, and we went to work without a guard. Well, he got into a real gulag-type camp, where the people went to work with guards in the quarries-- in stone quarries, among others. That particular fellow, who had been the chief of that restaurant chain for which I worked in Tarnopol for a while, he had military boots-- high boots, new ones-- and a military belt. He exchanged that with the commander of the jail for being assigned to the kitchen. And that's what saved him. Many of them didn't survive there. That was-- that was tough work. There was a lot of in-fighting going on, because the guards were stealing and some of the-- similar, like, in the concentration camps, some of the foremen or kind of capos-- they didn't call them, in Russia, that-- were involved in trades and stealing and some of oppression of some of the other guys. They had worked in job assignments, and so on. And some of the people really suffered. And a lot of people died in those [? stone quarries. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Good decision that you and your father didn't go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good decision that we didn't go. Good decision that we didn't go. They didn't-- want them to separate, because they knew families will have a hard time to escape. And if you are a single person, you are taking a risk, much easier.

speaker: interviewer
text: You won't take-- I won't take a risk and leave my father alone, there. And to go together with my father, who was, from my point of view as a 20-year-old or 19-year-old-- an elderly fellow. OK? I wouldn't have dared to take him because he wouldn't be able to do so. So that was the reasoning why they separated those so-called bachelors. What date were you discharged from the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was discharged from the army March of 1945. It was, I think, on the papers it was the 12th that I-- in, in reality, it was about the 15th of March, or 14th of March, something like that, from a unit in the border guards in a resort in the mountains in Lower Silesia, where, for the last few months, I had a rather pleasant service. I had been in a job which had been really was designed for a commissioned officer, and I was about two months away from getting a commission. They had a rule at the time-- if somebody serves six months in that job assigned for a commissioned officer, you could pass an examination. I wasn't eager to. [LAUGHING]

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to get out. Do you know that's 51 years ago this time of the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it 1946?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1945-- it was in 1946. Sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's 50 years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: 50 years ago, about this time, as we sit here.

speaker: interviewer
text: As we sit here, today, on March 14th, 1996.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. About 50 years ago. I'm sorry I said 1945. The war just-- on 9th, 9th of May, the war ended. It was 1946, I misspoke. And that's when I got out. And I was mighty glad. I had the offer to, from somebody, one of the officers said, would you like to join the secret police? And I said, no.
